text: Welcome to Fragmented, a software developer podcast where we talk about building good software and becoming better developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: My name's Don Felker.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm Kaushik Gopal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Welcome to the show.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Welcome back to the show, everybody.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Today's just me, Don.

text: We're missing Kaushik this week.

text: He's a little bit tied up and busy with some other stuff, but he'll be back soon enough.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: On today's show, I wanted to talk to you about the best way to learn a new technology, namely a programming language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This is something software developers do occasionally.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's not something we do every single day, but it is something that we do occasionally.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We eventually get used to working with one programming language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Maybe that's our first one that we've learned or second or third, but eventually we do need to pick up another language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Why is that?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, because as software developers, we don't exist purely in one domain.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, for example, we might write an application and it needs to write data to a database.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, we have to learn SQL to interact with a database typically.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I'm not gonna get into the argument of if SQL is really a programming language, I'm not going to do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm just saying in general, if you have to learn new technology, it's something we just encounter.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, today's conversation and so forth is gonna revolve around learning a traditional programming language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: or even you could talk about even a different framework for that nature.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's say that you are an Android developer and you wanted to learn how to build an iOS app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, that's a completely different framework.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's a completely different language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Everything's gonna be brand new.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And how do you go about doing that?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this conversation also applies to anyone who's interested in
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: perhaps learning how to build something in a completely different environment and framework than they're used to.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you're a mobile developer and you're interested in learning about the backend, maybe you wanna know how to build an API.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, how do you do that?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do you use something like Node or do you gonna use Rails or Python or is it Java or Kotlin?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All of those are valid answers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There is no wrong answer, so I want to make that clear initially.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I'm going to assume that you have an idea of something that you would like to build in a particular language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Not something you'd like to build, but you have an idea of a particular language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: that you would like to learn and so forth.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: How do you do that?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, first of all, you need to understand the basics of the language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's just gonna be the programming language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's gonna be plain and simple what you need to understand first before we can really get into the meat and potatoes of what I'm gonna talk about here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, for example, let's say that you're a Java developer and you wanna learn Kotlin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, you need to understand the fundamentals of Kotlin and how Kotlin works from the ground up, what the syntax looks like, how you declare variables and functions.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you'll need to learn that first.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And wherever you do that is where you do that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's assume now that you have already determined that you would like to build something and you want to learn a language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So let's go with the Android to iOS example.

text: You want to build an iOS app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Okay.

text: So first of all, you need to understand Swift probably.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So maybe you've already went and watched a couple of courses on the Swift programming language and you kind of understand it a little bit, but you want to build an iPhone app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So to do that, there's a couple of things that you need to keep in mind here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that's what we're going to talk about here.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So the first thing that you're going to need to keep in mind is you're going to need some type of goal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's number one, find a goal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that goal I recommend is going to be choosing a simple problem domain.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what does that mean?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: A problem domain is a problem that you're trying to solve and you need to choose one that's simple and you have memorized.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Without a shadow of a doubt, you understand how it works top to bottom.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what is maybe a simple domain?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, these are my examples that I actually use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's two examples I always rely on and I either choose either one of them and you can steal these if you want.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Number one is I'll either choose to build a note-taking app
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and a note-taking app is simple.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All it does is it allows you to create a note that has a title, that's text, has content, that's text, and it has a created date when the note was created, and then it has a updated at field, which is when the note was last updated.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if I created it yesterday and I added some more notes today, it would say that it was created yesterday and that it was updated today.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, each time I create a new note, there's going to be a new record inserted into some type of data store somewhere with those fields in it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I got four columns, title, content, created at, and updated at.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Pretty simple.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's option number one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, option number two is a calorie or protein tracker.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so that's very simple as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Every day, perhaps I would like to keep track of how many calories I want or the number of protein grams of protein I've eaten in a day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This app would be very simple.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's a simple input box, and you could have it except a number.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It also has a created at and updated at fields, which are going to be dates.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then every single day, I could enter in how many calories I ate that day or the number of protein grams I've eaten that day.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Whichever one you'd like to go with protein or calories, it's up to you.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, you might be thinking, like, these are really stupid apps, Don.

text: What a stupid idea.

text: Why would I build this such a dumb app?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I want to go do something cool with, like, machine learning and all this other cool stuff.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The reason why I'm telling you to use a simple problem domain is because...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: you don't wanna be having to learn the problem domain as you're learning a programming language.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The goal here is to remove complexity so you can focus on the learning environment.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the learning environment is gonna be you building something with that technology.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So you don't wanna be getting in the way of yourself.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if I have to learn about some brand new problem domain that I'm not an expert in, most likely I'm going to make mistakes and then possibly confuse myself
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: in the process of programming, as well as the process of learning the program in the new framework.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So with a note taking app, we all know how that works very simply.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And what's a note consist of a title usually, and some content where you can go open up a document in Microsoft word, Google docs, wherever, and you can put the date there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: the title of your note, and you could type it in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then tomorrow you could open that same document, add another new line, add the date, title, and content.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And you can create your notes right there and keep them there forever.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there's hundreds of thousands of people that do exactly that right now.

text: That's okay.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That works.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're not trying to replace that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: We're trying to just use that problem domain as a way for us to learn.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Well, when you start getting into APIs, things are a little bit different because APIs just kind of exist in their endpoints that are on the web and need to be authenticated and unauthorized to perform actions on an API.

text: So most likely you're probably gonna need to create the concept of users and user accounts.

text: People will need to be able to create a user account.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They'll need to be able to sign in.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And if it's through an API, these are going to be stateless, meaning that there's no session or anything like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And so you need some way to be able to pass back and forth your authentication and authorization mechanism of some sort.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And usually that's done through a token.

text: And these will be done through something known as JOTS, which is kind of the standard at this point in time.

text: And these are JWT.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So go to JWT.io, you'll see basically the spec and all the information and an encoder and decoder of how to decrypt and encrypt JWTs.

text: So let's go ahead and really kind of recap this just to kind of wrap this up.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: When you're learning a new technology, the first thing that you're going to want to do is choose a simple problem domain.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you don't have one, choose one.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The two that I recommend are a simple note-taking app or a protein or calorie tracker app.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Both of these have minimal fields.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: and they both include dates in them so you can start performing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As you add features to your app, you'll have, you can start doing date math and kind of do some cool stuff to see, build little reports inside of your app or something like that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it gives you options to further your application.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As well, one of the things I didn't mention about dates
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: is it gives you an opportunity to say, hey, how do I automatically update the update of that field when this record's updated?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Is it inside of a transaction?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Do I do it in the database?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So it kind of gives you some options to learn in each framework.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So number two.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't worry about the best practices.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, leave the best practices in the back of your mind.

text: Now, if you do need to learn how to use a map or whatever, then use that.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So that's number two.

text: Don't worry about best practices.

text: Okay.

text: Cool.

text: Great.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So start small and stay smaller.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just small incremental chunks.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't boil the ocean.

text: Number four, just get it to work.

text: That's really the big one here.

text: Just get it to work.

text: And that's kind of just builds on the previous example.

text: You just want to build things and get it to work.

speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So number four, build something and just get it to work.
